title: KPMG is a challenging workplace that will help you grow to help you understand the profession more. You are often given opportunities to grow and try new things. Additionally, they are supportive of you getting your CPA, even providing Becker materials. The pay is fairly competitive and you have a chance to make great connections. pros: KPMG is a challenging workplace that will help you grow to help you understand the profession more. You are often given opportunities to grow and try new things. Additionally, they are supportive of you getting your CPA, even providing Becker materials. The pay is fairly competitive and you have a chance to make great connections. Generally, you are working with great peers who are quick to help if you are struggling to grasp a task. A big 4 firm that has a lot of respect and will look spectacular on your resume.
cons: Management clearly decides who is their favorite and pour endlessly into them. This is amplified when you are paired with management who may not show any favoritism towards you. It's also clear that some senior members of the team don't actually understand aspects of the audit as if you ask a question, they cannot provide any insight into a solution. Sometimes, the most help you can get when a client is giving you a hard time is "do the best you can", which shows a clear lack of backbone for management to actually support junior team members. Busy seasons can be mentally taxing, having to work 70- 80 hour work weeks.

title: Big 4 experience can give you a nice breadth of client and skills knowledge, and acclimates you to pretty brutal hours/working conditions, which can be a tremendous advantage down the road. Benefits are also great for lower-level employees. There's a prescribed promotion timeline, provided you prove your worth, so there's some stability built into the job.
pros: Big 4 experience can give you a nice breadth of client and skills knowledge, and acclimates you to pretty brutal hours/working conditions, which can be a tremendous advantage down the road. Benefits are also great for lower-level employees. There's a prescribed promotion timeline, provided you prove your worth, so there's some stability built into the job.
cons: Training is awful, and by design; all the company needs are warm bodies for a few years, so they see little point in training actual accountants. If your senior is a clueless tyrant to you, chances are that's how they were treated as an associate. Hours can be nightmarish during busy season, but it really depends on the team/client. They expect a majority to leave for industry within 3-5 years, and when this doesn't happen according to their expectations, layoffs occur. Big 4 seems hell-bent on offshoring associate work despite all the quality problems this causes downstream (as an associate, a significant portion of your job will be managing the work of offshore teams). They are also hot to trot on Gen AI without having much of an idea how it will integrate into their current business model in a sustainable way.

**Forced RTO policy of 3 days a week (Even if you are on an engagement with those in different office locations) is both pointless and ableist, as many excellent employees with issues such as those with neurodivergence or physical health issues thrive when working remotely.

**Office setup is not conducive to forced RTO. Tiny and cramped, hot-desking office full of distractions. This setup is only being utilized to cut costs and to funnel more of the profits to the partner level, not because anyone likes it. It is a de-humanizing and classist setup in which the only person who has their own reserved office is the office managing partner. 

**Firm does not do a good job of ensuring first-year staff are getting the support they need from second/third year associates, and senior associates. 

**Firm has been regularly laying off staff since 2022 under the excuse of low levels of attrition, even though the real reason is to cover for ineffective partners who are failing to grow the business. Their solution after they fail to obtain new business is to cut lower-level staff so that they can keep their bonuses while mismanaging the office.
